description: Feudal and administrative territories defined by who rules them.
regions: Kingdom
A sovereign land of bannered castles and ancient crowns, its borders shaped by generations of war and treaty.
Empire
Sprawling dominion stitched together from conquered peoples, held by iron will and roads that never end.
Duchy
A noble's personal domain, often a day's ride across and fiercely defended.
March
Fortified borderland held by a military lord, always braced for the next incursion.
County
Patchwork farmland and quiet villages governed by a count's seal and seasonal courts.
Barony
A small lord's grant of tower, field, and forest, won through loyalty or blood.
Shire
Gentle countryside of hedgerows and market towns, governed more by custom than by sword.
Province
A broad administrative sweep of land, often spanning several cities and diverse peoples.
Territory
Claimed land whose borders exist more on parchment than in the memory of those who live there.
Realm
All the lands answering to a single throne, from heartland keeps to distant frontier posts.
Dominion
A region held under firm, unquestioned authority, its people shaped by the ruler's will.
Domain
The personal holdings of a lord, where every field and road bears their mark.
Principality
A proud, compact state ruled by a prince, often perched among mountains or along a contested coast.
Marquisate
A border lord's grant of wild frontier land, rewarded for standing watch against what lies beyond.
Canton
A small, fiercely independent territory carved from highland valleys and mountain passes.
Prefecture
An orderly administrative division where appointed governors enforce distant imperial law.
Bailiwick
A magistrate's jurisdiction of courts and toll roads, where law reaches every crossroads.
Commanderie
A territory controlled by a military order, its stone keeps doubling as barracks and chapels.
Freehold
Land owned outright and beholden to no lord, a rare and jealously guarded liberty.
Protectorate
A weaker land sheltered under a stronger power's banner, sovereign in name but not in fact.
Viceroyalty
A distant province governed by a crown-appointed viceroy, ruling in the monarch's absent name.
Archduchy
A great ducal territory of considerable prestige, often rivaling lesser kingdoms in wealth and arms.
Electorate
A territory whose lord holds the sacred privilege of choosing the next emperor.
Grand Duchy
A vast ducal holding of many vassals, its court glittering with ambition and old rivalries.
Palatinate
A privileged territory whose lord wields near-royal authority within their own borders.
Commonwealth
A territory founded on shared governance, where councils and charters hold more power than any single throne.
Manor
A landed estate of hall, mill, and surrounding fields, the smallest unit of feudal power.
Fiefdom
Land granted in exchange for sword-service, its boundaries measured in a knight's obligations.
District
A clearly bounded administrative division, defined by function rather than by history or blood.
Ward
A walled quarter of a city, each with its own gate, character, and local pride.
Quarter
A city district known by the trade or people that give it life and noise.
Precinct
A tightly defined area within a larger territory, often set apart for a specific purpose.
Zone
A marked stretch of land distinguished by particular laws, hazards, or forbidden purpose.
